sentences.text: Yeah, I was saying the worst incident which happened to me, besides being starved, and starving of hunger, and all the rest of it, one morning, there was a commotion going on.

And like, I'd just managed to drag myself through the door, and I saw-- because we didn't have plates, or soup-- soup plates, or anything, we just had tins with a little handle, like painters have, and I saw these people [? running ?] in with this liquid.
I went and got my tin, and I went out and I got this liquid-- this soup.
What it was, I don't even know what it was.
Suddenly, they've-- they heard the commotion, the kapos, and they come into the block and they started beating people.
I think about three or four got killed from beating over the heads.
I put my tin [BACKROUND NOISE] between two bunks on the floor.
I could-- I was thin, I was little.
I could climb under the bed.
I went into-- into my bunk and I pretended I was asleep.
I was shuddering, I was tittering.
If they found-- If they would have just asked me, where's my tin, I would have had to say, oh, here it is.
I wouldn't have had enough sense to say, I don't know, somebody stole it or something.
Luckily for me, I wouldn't be here today to tell the tale, they-- they thought I was asleep.
They never bothered me.
And when I think about that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I just shudder.
And that is the worst thing which happened to me whist in Belsen.

I'm not even counting the fact that I was having to do what I did, as I was explaining before, about fighting for a few potato peelings, and for maybe some stale bread somebody chucked from a window or whatever.

Then, as I said, the story was we eventually got a little bit stronger, those who managed-- in my case, as I was saying to you before, in my case, I was preferring to eat a potato or a lump of hard bread, rather than, say, tins of whatever they were giving you and all the rest of it.
And that is-- maybe, it wasn't time for me to go.
Maybe the Lord up there didn't want me-- it wasn't my time to go.
And that's how I survived.
And it wasn't-- Eventually, as the rumors went round, every day-- was about-- must have been sometimes-- August.
Oh, by the way, forget-- I forgot to tell this story.
Was-- It was about August time.
Five of us went out for a walk.
We finished up at this suite of fruit trees.
We filled our pockets up with apples, plums, and a German come out and he shouted, get off my land, or some to that effect.
And one of my friends put his hand in his pocket, and with his finger, he pointed, get out, leave, get away from here or you'll get shot.
Whilst all this was going on, we were laughing and picking this fruit.
Now, I'm talking about, now, two and a half, three and a half months after liberation.
Before you could say, good morning, two Jeeps with British Tommies surrounded us.
It was about-- as I said, there was four, or five, or six of us.
We were taken to the local nick, put in behind bars, questions about, where is this gun?
And we tried to explain, there was no gun.
I mean, in our own Yiddish.
I-- I-- I can't swear for certain, but I can 75% that one or two of the bigger ones got beatings from the British soldiers, asking, where is this gun?
And I may also add, on the way to this-- to this nick, we were passing a hairdresser's, and I just had about a half an inch or an inch of hair.
And I was thinking, oh, they're not gonna cut my hair  off, they're not gonna cut my hair off.
So I-- I was jerking so bad, they're going to do something to us and cut my hair off.
We passed-- we just walked past this hairdresser's, and we were kept three, four, or five days in this police station.
Eventually, we were take in front of an officer with a German lady interpreter, which was killing me-- and I'm sure the others felt the same-- saying we cannot-- we could not find the gun.
If you appear in front of us again, you'll be locked up for a long time.
That's how it was later-- many years later, and that is just a little experience, what happened to us after the war.
And gradually, as I said, slowly, you found out that people were registering to go to Palestine one day, America another day, Britain another day.
And every-- every other day, I registered to go somewhere else.
And I registered with two of my best friends to come to England.
As I was the only one who was under 16, I was the only one chosen.
And I came over to England in a transport September, 1945, to Southampton.
We arrived at dusk or 10 o'clock at night, about, as I said, 29th or 30th of September.
There was a double-decker bus waiting for us.
Stupid things you remember.
They had to carry the luggage upstairs, because we were all frightened to go upstairs in case the bus topples over.
That was so [INAUDIBLE].
And we finished up in-- in [?
Dudley ?] House, Southampton in a hostel.

I was there about four or six weeks, then we were taken all the way to Scotland, and a little village called Cardross, where I met up the boys who came from Theresienstadt to-- and then on to Windermere.
And from Windermere-- when they close Windermere, they all-- they all finished up in this Cardross in this hostel.
It's a lovely house, far-- along-- all the way uphill from the village.
And we began to live a little.
It was a lovely summer.
We used to have two bikes, two bikes between eight boys.
So whoever managed to get the bike first-- it was always me-- that's why I broke a leg, and I broke an-- an ankle, and my wrist, and off we went for the day.
So when there was any lessons going, I was hardly there.
I fell off my bike and we used to sort of go down this hill.
And suddenly, a herd of cows appeared in front of you, braked my brake, finished up in the fields.
And you know, a  little-- we started-- as I said, we start to live, and we sort of, you know, we were taken to, like, places, to the cinema.
We were taken to [?
Button ?] Barton.
We began to, sort of being taken to Burton's to be fitted out in suits.
And you know, when you're 16, 16 and 1/2, I had a blue suit and a brown suit, exactly the same, same stripes and everything.
And my ambition was to have the same in a gray.
And I finished up having the same in a gray, exactly the same stripe and everything.
I thought I was-- don't ask.
But then, obviously, as soon as we arrived in England, I started writing home.
I wrote home for about 20 months.
I heard nothing.
I understand that my brother was taken to Russia, to Moscow.
And when they announced, the Russians announced certain times, certain dates that, from that and that day, to the that and that date, Hungarians can go home, and then the Romanians, and so on.
I don't know whether it is true or not.
I believe my brother was taken by a Russian lady doctor who lost her son in the war.
And she more or less took him home to adopt him.
But when his chance came, he-- he got this travel permit and he came home.
After about--
--eight-- To-- to our village.
About 18 to 22 a month, I don't exactly know, suddenly, I get a letter from him saying, [?
Shlomo, ?] what you doing in England?
I said, I came over in children transport, with so many boys and girls, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
And then he informed me that my sister, Fage was left in Auschwitz, is dead.
Because they took everybody off on marches.
They took all these people off on marches and thousands died from hunger and thirst.
They wouldn't even let you pick up a bit of snow to drench your thirst.
But my sister was left for dead.
When the Russians came in, they found her swollen, double her size, her legs and everything else.
And they took her to hospital, and they brought her round, and she survived.
So I learned now that I got brother and I've got a sister.
Then a--
I learned that through letter.
No, 20 months later.
I didn't know-- didn't know that anybody survived.
I never went home.
Some of my friends went back to Poland, and back to their countries and-- to see whether anybody lived.
I never went home.
I was too stupid, too young, didn't even think about it.
And then we found that my sister, the young sister who lived away from home-- remember me telling you at the beginning of the film?
Mmm.
She came home one day and told my father she was engaged.
She was only about 17.
The way I see it, my father said, yes, over my dead body.
So back-- back she went, to this aunt she was staying with in another part of-- two or three villages-- two or three towns away.
About 14 or 18 got themselves together and they call themselves halutzim, and they run across the border to Russia.
The Russians caught them, and they kept them in prison for three months as spies.

When they quizzed them and found out that they were only children, they didn't-- they were not spies, [INAUDIBLE] my sister finished up working all through the war in a-- in a Russian hospital, scrubbing and do that and the other.
She finished up-- They found-- They found that she finished up in Vienna.
She met her husband in Vienna, and they had-- and they had a lovely marriage.
They got married in Vienna and under a chuppah and everything.
So from thinking I was on my own only, we were lucky enough to have-- have four survivors.
I lost my father, my mother, [BACKROUND NOISE] my elder sister, my younger brother.
My elder sister, I-- I heard she was in Belsen, in the typhus part.
They cordoned off this part of the camp.
And I went-- Somebody told me, you know, your sister is in there, there.
I went.
They wouldn't-- I mean, I couldn't talk to the guard at the gate.
He wouldn't let me in anyhow.
And that I heard-- I don't even know today-- to this day, whether she was in Belsen and she died there, or whether it was just somebody's-- a rumor.
That's all I can tell you about her.
And as I said, we were lucky that four out of a family of eight survived.
Some of my friends.
I've got who are-- who were 10 or more, they went back to Latvia, and Poland, and all these places.
They found no one.
They were just on their own.
But as far-- that is, you see?
So it was just a matter of luck.
It-- The whole thing, from beginning to end, was luck.
If they came along one day and they wanted a 100 to the gas chambers, or a 100 for very, very hard work or-- or whatever, you happened to be 105, you was lucky enough.
You lived another day.
And from another day, went into another day.
But those 100, you never seen again, never heard of them again.
Just a [NON-ENGLISH], an example, you see?
[SIGHS]
I've made a film before, and I cried my eyes out in that film.
But I seem to be immune to it now.
Things are getting really in a state.
My son got married two and a half years ago.
I've got a photo of 12 of us on this particular photo.
Four of them passed away in the last two and a half years.
And you get immune to things, you-- you-- in the camps as well.
You get used to the idea to get beatings.
You get used to being shotted upon.
You get used to being number.
It wasn't a matter of having to get, you just got used to it.
And that's how it went all through.
And we were lucky, because we were only in about 13 and 1/2 months, whereas a lot of my friends, as I said before, were in it a long time.
So from going back to where I left off, my brother suddenly start writing to me.
And I wrote back, and said, you know, from here, came over on child transport and all that.
It was about-- I-- I learned years later that he went to White Alps in the snows to Palestine, and he got in in 1947.
I went to Israel the very first time in '55, which was 11 and 1/2 years since I saw them last.
When I saw them, we were all grown up with our own children.
Whereas, 11 years, 11 and 1/2 years, when you are 40 or 50 means nothing, There I was, going on a boat from south of France on [INAUDIBLE].
It took about five days to arrive.
There was salty showers.
I love the sun.
I was sitting in the-- in the shower and sunbathing, in the shower and sunbathing.
By the time I got there, I was as black as a man from Africa.
In the best of times, we wouldn't have known one another.
I arrived in Haifa, and there were thousands of people waving, and I was waving back.
And gradually, you pull-- something pulls you.
And there I was, I mean, I had photos they sent me in the past, and we met up.
My brother took out-- My brother took my sister, and she had two kids from Romania, 1950.
And my other sister went from Vienna in 1952.
No, I'm telling a lie.
She was there while-- while the Arabs attacked, and she had twins.
And my brother was managing to get through to her with food, and water, and all everything else.
And as I said, when we first met up after 11 and 1/2 years, we were-- we was lovely.
We were crying.
We were laughing.
We were joking, and we were strangers.
Because there I was, when I last saw him, about 14, and next time, I was 26.
And the-- the reunion, what we had is-- I wish it on all the people who lost family, would have had that, what we had.
And gradually, I worked myself up.
They started deciding that, you know, being in England, that we were reaching 16 and 1/2.
They started asking what jobs we want to do, and so on, and so on.
Only-- No.
Never, never, never.
Only the lessons what we supposed to have had whilst I was in Scotland.
Never had a day's schooling.
I learned to read and write.
I don't write very well, even now, but I can-- I can read very well.
I love reading.
And for some reason or other, I always finish up going to the library, bringing back more books.
My wife used to say, leave them alone, leave them alone.
Don't-- And I said, yeah, OK, OK, OK.
But every time, I come back.
I learned a lot from what was going on under my own nose, in Buchenwald.
There was a book I read two or three times called It was written by the leader of the resistance in Paris.
And what-- I mean, I was at Buchenwald.
I didn't know what was going on.
But he-- What I read, what he went through, and what happened to him, my hair stood up on end, you know?
Because, as I said, I didn't know what was going on, what was happening.
And by reading books, I sort of educated myself, and as I said, I never had a lesson in my life.
And slowly, I finished up-- sort of, they asked you, what job-- jobs do you want to?
I had a half an address from America, because I had my aunt-- my father's brother-- my father's sister there.
And they decided that he was a jeweler, so they decided I should go to [INAUDIBLE] [?
Garden, ?] learn to become a jeweler, which I was more than happy.
It wasn't until many years later, I think it was some-- somewhere in the-- in '61, '62, I went to America for the first time.
[INAUDIBLE] was 40-- 55-pound return.
And I met up with my cousins.
My auntie had died by then.
No, I'm telling-- you see?
I don't remember.
My auntie-- I've got a photo of my auntie with me on-- by her big, long car.
I understand that, obviously, I stayed with them, and I met a lot of the boys who were originally in England, and they were in-- in America.
And I went to my auntie, and I had some of my friends.
They were invited to dinner.
There was about 14 or 16 people.
And I-- I was talking for a change.
I always talk.
And suddenly, it all went quiet.
And I said, what's that?
What's happened?
Why has everybody gone quiet?
We do love the way you talk English, Steve.
That was-- and my-- my accent is, I-- I claim, I was taught English in Scotland by German refugees who came over before the war.
But really, I never had a proper lesson.
I might have stayed once or twice, with a blackboard outside, I might say, a lovely summer, outside.
And [BACKROUND NOISE] it wasn't until many years later that I found out that this-- what's her name's-- my-- that-- you know, I'm-- I'm-- I'm mixing myself up.
We were talking at this table, and they said to me-- he's probably falling asleep-- they said to me, Steve, how come you're a jeweler?
I said, well, I was going to come to America.

I was going-- I was-- My uncle was being a jeweler, and I was-- thought, if I come here, I will-- I'll be able to work for him, or if not for him, he might know somebody else who-- He said, what are you talking about?
Your uncle was never a jeweler.

He wasn't the [? smartest. ?] I said, it just shows you by shear fate, I became a jeweler, because they made out at the Jewish Refugee Committee, that I-- that my uncle was a jeweler, from the half-address I gave him.
So who knows?
I might have had 1,000 people working for me, or been a-- a-- a tailor instead.
And that-- that this part of my story.
But what happened, about five, six years ago, my brother took his two strappy sons to Romania to show them where we were born.
And then they came home.
Why?
Auschwitz.
Unbeknown to him, they had a jar and they filled it with ashes, and they didn't tell him till they got back to Israel what they'd done.
So that-- My brother went to the [?
RAF ?] in [?
Rehovot-- ?] because my brother lives [INAUDIBLE] here, and told him what happened.
And they wanted-- they asked whether he could have a [NON-ENGLISH] for our parents, and my brother, and sister whom we lost.
The [?
RAF ?] gave his permission.
So we all paid so much towards a [NON-ENGLISH], and I went with my wife to Israel, and we had a stone setting.
There was about 50-plus people.

And I feel that this is a very good thing, what we've done, because every time I go to Israel, I go to the-- visit the holy grounds, and I say, Kaddish and-- when I get there for-- and then when-- before I leave.
And also this stone setting was all-- all in [NON-ENGLISH].
It didn't touch me at all.
I didn't understand what it was all about.
I went back a few days later with my sister-in-law, and my wife and my brother, and I said to him, now explain to me in English, what does it mean?
And when she explained word-for-word, four-- all four of us cried our eyes out.
For 15 minutes, I was sobbing.
I could not stop.
And I feel that's the best thing I've done, almost the best thing I've done since the liberation.
But I don't know whether other people told you this story.
We-- About two years ago, all the 45-- I belong to the 45 Aid Society, which is the boys and girls who came over after the war, who are now helping those who need.
We have decided to have a Torah written on behalf of us.
We all contributed X amount of money.
Last year, we had a lovely-- the guy who actually wrote the Torah is one-- a son of our-- one of our boys.
And he had unfinished letters, unfinished words.
He asked all of us, in turn, the names of our fathers.
Like, my father's name was David.
So he had a [? dalit ?] and he directed my hand to finish this word which was missing, the D, the [? dalit ?] in front.
And we had a lovely, lovely day.
It was in the-- one of the shuls in [INAUDIBLE].
They're making a plaque, which is going to be hung in [INAUDIBLE] Shul with all our parents' names probably sometime this year.
